videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Porto and in this video we're going to show you the best things to do in the city and around it. It's a really romantic city with a ton of history and of course some really tasty wine. So I hope you enjoy this video. We are Zac and Ine, full-time digital nomads from the United States and Belgium. We met while studying abroad in 2018 and have been traveling the world together ever since. We're here to inspire your next adventure, whether it's hiking in the Canadian Rockies, a road trip through the US, or backpacking across Africa. On our channel, you'll find unfiltered videos of hiking guides and the best things to do in destinations around the world. Located in northwest Portugal along the Douro River, Porto is the second largest city in the country. The city played an important role in Portugal's history and was a flourishing trading port from early on. But only in the 18th century did Porto really establish itself as a key trading hub. This was thanks to the export of port wine to England and the rest of the world. While porto is still famous for this type of wine, there are also a ton of other reasons why this is a must-visit travel destination. From its stunning old town to its authentic cuisine and romantic viewpoints, Porto has so much to offer. In this video, we highlight our favorite activities in and around the city. Whether you're religious or not, visiting the churches of Porto is a great way to get to know the city's history and architecture. There are dozens of beautiful churches in Porto but a few stand out, such as Clerigos Church and Tower, St. Francis Church, the Church of Carmo, and the Porto Cathedral. If you only want to visit one, we recommend the Porto Cathedral as it's one of the city's oldest and most historical monuments. One of the most visited attractions in all of Porto is undoubtedly the enchanting Lello Bookshop. The building dates back to 1906 and is often nicknamed "the most beautiful bookshop in the world" due to its blend of different architectural designs. The unique wooden staircase, ceiling piping, and stained glass skylight are just mesmerizing. However, the shop is small and the queue to get inside can be very long. We recommend buying a skip the line ticket voucher which comes with a free book. In this way, you'll potentially avoid hours of waiting. Apart from its traditional port wine, there's a lot of food to experience in Porto. You can try local and national specialties, such as "tripas", which is a hearty stew made with white beans and beef stomach. You can also try cod fish and grilled sardines. But our favorite regional dish was "francesinha", which is made up of various kinds of meat inside bread with melted cheese on top and sometimes an egg. So this is one of the most traditional Porto dishes and it's called "francesinha" and you can try it anywhere in any restaurant in Porto. But the recommendation from our guide was to try it at Café Santiago, so let's try it. At number seven, we have the riverfront promenade along the Douro. It's one of the liveliest parts of Porto and a great place to walk, dine, and sightsee. Here, you'll observe beautiful views of the river and the iconic Dom Luís 1 bridge. When we were there, it was the busiest day of the year: St John's Fest. At this festival, the city commemorates their Patron Saint on the 23rd of June, with live music, drinking, grilled sardines, lots of dancing, hitting each other with plastic hammers, and tons of fireworks. If you have the chance to visit Porto on that day, we highly recommend it! Another great thing to do in Porto is to embark on a classic Six Bridges Cruise on the Douro River. From the water, you can admire the beautiful landscapes and red roof buildings of Porto as well as Vila Nova de Gaia. All while learning about the historic bridges that connect both cities. The crews will even take you near the seafront, where the river pours into the ocean. Boat tours leave from the riverfront in Porto and last about 50 minutes. To book your tour, click the link in the description below. Similar to Lisbon, Porto is a hilly city with many breathtaking viewpoints. The Vitória viewpoint is among the best known ones with views over the Old Town and the river. Other beautiful viewpoints include the street next to the Porto Cathedral or from the Cathedral itself, as well as the Virtudes Promenade and Park, and from the waterfront of Vila Nova de Gaia. Our favorite sunset view was from the Ribeira viewpoint. We're right now walking the Luís 1 Bridge, and if you cross it you actually come into a new city, which is called Vila Nova de Gaia. And from there you can watch the sunset over the city of Porto, which is incredible. Located near the Spanish border, the Peneda-Gerês National Park is a two-hour drive from Porto. It's the oldest protected area and the only national park in Portugal, covering about 695 square kilometers. From authentic mountain villages to amazing landscapes with numerous viewpoints and waterfalls, this place is truly breathtaking. The park can be easily explored by car as many roads connect the remote villages and beautiful viewpoints. However, the best thing to do is to get out in nature and take it all in. You can find a link to a 4x4 day tour from Porto in the description below. One of the best things to do near Porto is to visit Portugal's most beautiful wine country. The Douro wine valley sits on the banks of the Douro River, upstream from Porto and almost to the border of Spain. This region is where the famous port wine is produced and it's known as the first demarcated wine region in the world. But besides its wines, this valley is also worth visiting for its incredible landscapes, nature, and history. We spent two days in Peso da Régua and enjoyed wine tasting, hiking, and learning all about the region. You can also do a day trip to this region by embarking on a boat cruise from Porto. Find more info about that via the link in the description. If you don't have time to go to the Douro Valley, there's also a closer option. While less idyllic, Vila Nova de Gaia is also known for its port wine. In fact, all of the port wine that's produced in the upper Douro Valley would historically be transported here in barrels on traditional boats. This is why the riverbanks of Porto today are filled with wine cellars and tasting rooms. Most wine cellars offer wine tastings and guided tours, during which they'll explain more of the history. But besides the wine cellars, this waterfront is also home to numerous restaurants and offers some of the best views of Porto. One of the best ways to get to know Porto and the history of Portugal in general is by taking a free walking tour. During a two and a half hour guided tour, you'll see the main highlights of the city like São Bento train station, Café Majestic, Aliados Avenue and much more. But you'll also get some recommendations for where to eat, go out, and just have fun. The tour we did leaves a few times per day from the Liberty Square. Find more info about it via the link in the description below. So my name is Jorge and I'm from a small town very near Porto, actually it's called Sever do Vouga. In your opinion why do you think people should visit Porto? Oh wow, I think this speaks for itself right? And this is a very very beautiful town not only because it's a historical city, but because it's very much alive. This is basically where Portugal was born. The city, the name of Porto, actually originated the name of Portugal. And that's it for our video on Porto. There are a few places we didn't mention that are definitely worth checking out. If you have the time for more information such as the best time to visit and to download the actual maps used in this video, check out our blog link in the description. We'll catch you on the next adventure.

Venice of Portugal this picturesque city is famous for its Charming canals colorful molero boats and stunning art nuuvo architecture hop on a relaxing boat tour through the canals gliding past vibrant houses and soaking in the unique views of the city and don't miss out on trying ovos moles a local sweet treat made from egg yolks and sugar a perfect start to your adventure next take off to gimar known as the birthplace of Portugal this historic city is where the country's first king aonso first was born begin your visit at the medieval gimari castle for panoramic views and a deep dive into Portugal's Rich [Music] history then explore the Palace of The Dukes of briganza a stunning 15th century residence packed with impressive art and antiques stroll through the Charming Old Town a UNESCO world heritage site and soak in the historical ambiance wrap up your day with a relaxing visit to Matos Beach a beautiful Coastal spot just a short drive from Porto it's the perfect place to unwind and reflect on your day's Adventures take a leisurely stroll along the Sandy Shores dip your toes in the Atlantic Ocean and watch the Surfers catch waves feeling hungry Matos is famous for its seafood restaurants where you can enjoy fresh delicious dishes right by the sea and that's a wrap on our 4-day adventure in and around Porto from the historic streets of the rera district to the Serene beauty of the Duro Valley we hope you've enjoyed exploring the best that this amazing region has to offer if you found this itinerary helpful make sure to subscribe to our Channel and share your own experiences and tips in the comments below safe travels and until next time keep exploring

videos.transcript: if you're planning a trip to Portugal or thinking about it I just got back from a 2-e trip and I'm going to go over my full itinerary because I think it was the perfect trip for firsttime visitors we saw a lot of the highlights with a few of the Hidden gems sprinkled in we covered some of the country's most famous spots from the historic beauty of Porto to the sundrenched beaches of the Algarve and lots in between so I'm going to share where we stayed and for how long what we did what was worth it and wasn't worth it and also what I would do differently the next time I go back I'll start with an overview of our schedule we flew into Porto and stayed four nights then went to Lisbon and stayed four nights there and then we went to the Algarve and stayed four nights there and then flew home from Pharaoh many people buy roundtrip flights to Lisbon which is usually the most affordable and convenient option however I'm so glad we planned our trip this way because we didn't have to do any backtracking or have any was Ed travel days I highly recommend doing it this way if you can we were able to do this because I actually found premium economy flights that were cheaper than economy seats so my daily drop Pro membership is definitely worth it I'll leave a link below if you want to check it out for yourself all right so we started in Porto and basically the way our schedule worked out was that we had about 2 and 1/2 days to explore the city I loved Porto so much it has this amazing mix of vibrant history old world architecture almost medieval esque and the food and wine scene is chef's kiss speaking of that we took a food tour on our first morning which was definitely the way to go reses we loved strolling the iconic neighborhoods like the rera district with its colorful narrow streets there's so many great viewpoints to explore lots of great wine bars the Maro doala was fun to visit this is the original Open Air Market where you can browse all kinds of vendors you can get a glass of wine while you shop and peruse it's just a fun and Lively atmosphere even if you don't plan to buy anything and of course you have to visit the Old Port Wine sellers do some tours some tastings it's really fun then on our last day we took a day trip to the Duro Valley the birthplace of port wine for me this was a highlight of the whole trip it's such a Scenic part of the country absolutely breathtaking we found this great small group tour and our guide was actually a Somalia he brought us to two wineries that were totally different from each other in the best way oh oh it's really [Music] cool we went on a boat ride along the Duro River and and then checked out some awesome viewpoints of the Valley's terraced Vineyards and gorgeous Landscapes honestly this is a must do if you come to Porto non-negotiable I would even say next time I come back to Portugal though I'm definitely saving at least two or three nights for the Duro Valley it was [Music] beautiful we booked this trip on get your guide so I will leave a link in the description below or you can also scan the QR code in Porto we stayed at an apartment hotel called Victoria's Terrace Apartments it had really good reviews and I know why now it's in the perfect location right on the edge of the Oldtown area so perfectly walkable to everywhere but nice and quiet at night and the view from our balcony was actually better than one of the Hidden Gem viewpoints that was just down the street from us so that was pretty cool and the couple that runs this place are so kind and friendly they give us all kinds of recommend for places nearby like we went to a photo show that was 2 minutes away and it was an unforgettable [Music] evening there's this awesome little Hidden Gem Terrace bar called rooftop Flores go there for Sunset bevies and there's also great restaurants within 5 or 10 minutes I really liked C Tina 32 oh and I cannot forget franchina this is a Porto specific food it's just something you have to try it's bread and two or three kinds of meat and there's cheese and an egg on top and a tomato beer sauce we found awesome ones at this restaurant that I cannot pronounce but their whole menu looked great as well overall I think 2 and 1/2 days in Porto was enough I would absolutely stay 3 or 4 days if I had more time but I think you do need at least one and a half to two full days to really experience the city plus a full day dedicated to the duroro valley on day five instead of taking the train straight to Lisbon we actually hired a driver and then that way we were able to turn the transfer day into a full sightseeing day and this was honestly one of the best decisions of our trip so I'll leave the link to vur where we found our guide and driver he picked us up from our hotel in Porto at 9:00 a.m. and then we arrived in Lisbon by 6:00 p.m. where we made three stops along the way first we stopped in AO which is known as the Venice of Portugal because of its canals and colorful boats it's a pretty little town as well with lots of beautiful buildings in art Nuvo style it's also known for a traditional Suite called ooes not sure how to pronounce it but something we learned was you always have to try the local pastry when you're in Portugal our next stop was nazer a coastal town famous for its massive waves that attract Surfers from all over the world usually from November to February is where you can see the highest waves on Earth sometimes reaching as high as 30 m while we weren't visiting in big wave season the viewpoints overlooking the ocean and the beach are absolutely breathtaking and definitely worth the stop the village itself is also quite picturesque and the pink marble cathedral was definitely one of the highlights finally we stopped in obidos a small medieval Town enclosed by Castle stone walls that you can actually walk on top of I loved this little town so much it's cobblestone streets flower lined alleys historic charm little shops it was just so adorable and it's also known for its Cherry lure Ginger which is served in little chocolate cups and there's little ginger stands every 20 or 30 feet they're only about one or one and a half years per shot so I definitely recommend trying out a few I would have loved to spend at least half a day here in obidos next time maybe even stay in the castle which is now actually a fourstar hotel so if you only have time for two stops I would probably skip AO and if I only had time to visit one place I would definitely choose obidos we then arrived in Lisbon where we checked into our hotel and stayed for the next 4 nights we stayed at this apart hotel called Ando living once again I booked it because of the amazing reviews and they were totally right this hotel was in the perfect location in the baija neighborhood so we were within walking distance or easy public transport to all the main attractions in the old neighborhoods it was also nice and quiet at night it was so clean new and modern reasonably priced and the little welcome package of wine and goodies was a lovely touch and literally around the corner was the Green Street area which is three blocks of little authentic bars and restant resturants that open up in the evening it's kind of similar to Pink street but the better more authentic Hidden Gem among locals so then we spent the next two full days exploring Lisbon on our first day we took a halfday tuck tuck tour this was a fantastic way to get introduced to the city our guide took us through Lisbon's famous neighborhoods Alama bisha bario elto and chiato Lisbon is also filled with viewpoints or Mirad duros and our guide took us to some of the best ones for the best views of the city he also took great photos of us which turned out to be some of my favorites from the whole trip we finished our tutic tour in bam home to the BM Tower built to defend Lisbon against possible attacks and invasions and it's one of the most unique monuments in Lisbon and there's nothing quite like it in other European cities as well there's the gonos monastery which is a gorgeous Portuguese Gothic building that was built in the 17th century this is also home to the birthplace of the famous pases de balm the original recipe of pastel denata everyone said these were the best of the best the Portuguese custard Tarts and yes I can confirm they are in A League of Their Own we booked this trip through get your guide so I'll leave the link below to the one that we booked or you can scan the QR code I also just want to say that none of this trip was sponsored we paid for everything ourselves from the hotels to the guides to the drivers to the restaurants everything Lisbon is a great City to just wander get lost take in the sights snap some fun photos and stop for food and bees along the way and that's exactly what we did we really liked the carel rooftop bar for Sunset as well and if you like espresso martinis you have to visit cassa do pastel deata leor for a special lisban Twist on this yummy cocktail [Music] we also took in another foto show the old neighborhoods here in Lisbon is where this originated foto is a traditional style of Portuguese music characterized by mournful tunes and lyrics often expressing a sense of longing Nostalgia or sadness I would say catching a photo show is a must do we chose to do dinner and the photo show at Bohemia LX but you can also find smaller venues and stop in and have maybe a glass of wine while you listen to the Beautiful music [Music] all right now here are a few things that I thought were a little overrated in lisbin first was the timeout Market to me this was just a food court but perhaps if you're in town for only a few hours maybe from a cruise and you want to try a bunch of different Portuguese Foods this would probably be a good stop I also wasn't crazy about pink Street maybe I'm too old it was loud busy and full of influencers and waiting in line for the Santa useda lift it was really cool to look at it's very interesting architecture for sure but you can actually find stairs to go up for free or you can also get a great view of it from the carel rooftop I probably could have used an extra day in Lisbon but because we saw Porto which I kind of preferred the more laid-back vibe there 2 days was enough for me in Lisbon I will say Lisbon was very busy when we were there in mid October so I can't even imagine how busy it gets in the summer months our last and Lisbon was dedicated to cintra a fairy tale Town nestled in the Hills about 45 minutes from the city this is one of the most popular day trips from Lisbon and a place that you absolutely have to see with your own eyes there are several stunning palaces to visit here and you won't have time to see them all in one day so you will have to choose we visited two of the most famous palaces starting with the colorful Penna Palace which looks like something straight out of a story book it's got colorful Towers an Eclectic [Music] architecture we then toured the intricate Kint de regula a romantic estate with beautiful gardens underground tunnels and the famous initiation well we booked a private tour and this was absolutely worth every penny because cintra is huge there is so much more than just a few palaces to visit our guide not only brought us everywhere but came with us and explained all the history and all the stories behind everything we were looking at I am so glad we hired a guide this whole area is breathtaking but also quite overwhelming especially with the crowds there were so many people without a guide that were kind of just aimlessly wandering around and a lot of them were constantly trying to listen into what our guide was sharing with us so I'll leave a link to the guide that we booked if you booked with him let him know Al Smalls sent you and if he's not available I also left links for a couple other highly rated guides and tours for our final leg of the trip we took a 45-minute flight from Lisbon to Pharaoh a city in the algar we got ourselves a rental car and then drove to caruo our home base for the last four nights we chose caruo because it's centrally located which made it really easy to explore the other towns beaches and hiking trails along the algar coast the town itself is super Charming with its whitewashed buildings and a beautiful Coastline we stayed at a six suet Guest House Bed and Breakfast called Kint do ores when I tell you it's one of the best accommodations I have ever stayed in I am not exaggerating it's run by the sweetest family who are so kind and full of great recommendations for what to do in the area each morning guests are treated to a massive breakfast spread and any type of coffee that you want there's a large heated pool with all kinds of room for sunbathing plenty of common areas to hang out and relax in and all the rooms have private patios with kitchens I will 100% be booking a vacation just to stay here again we only had four nights and I wish we had 10 more one thing we quickly realized after getting to the Algarve was that four nights was nowhere near enough but here were the highlights for us in that time one of our favorite spots was algar SEO this is a spot with Incredible rock formations and natural Gros all along the coast you can take some amazing photos and even Explore all the little hidden caves it's only 5 minutes from the hotel as well we also hiked 8 km of the seven hanging valleys Trail which offers some of the best coastal views in all of Portugal the trail runs along the cliffs above the ocean passing by Scenic viewpoints hidden Coes and secluded beaches this is a must do and was a highlight of the whole trip for me another highlight was kaying to the benel C caves we were able to get so much closer than the bigger boat tours we took a guided tour and we saw so many other caves while we kayaked along the coastline highly recommend doing this but it's definitely a bit of a workout so be prepared for that we spent some time visiting some of the most famous and beautiful beaches and postcard views near Lagos in albuera each beach in The Algarve has its own Vibe and so exploring a few of them really adds to the experience [Music] there's also a lot of cute towns and Villages all over I really loved farago an adorably picturesque town with whitewashed houses built in the Hillside and flowers all through the streets also a great spot for souvenir shopping overall it was an amazing trip and I think 12 days was a good amount of time to cover a lot of ground without feeling too rushed especially because we made the most of our travel days I went on this trip with my mom and sister and we all took one carry-on bag and one personal item each and that was definitely the way to go these are the bags that we used here that fit on all the airlines that we flew I'll leave links to the bags but I also made a video talking about them more in depth and we also used sling bags I used the large Sling from Nomad Lane my sister used the Metro Sling from monos and my mom used her belt bag from Lululemon each of these were perfect size and really nice and secure so I'll leave links to the those as well I also put this itinerary up on my travel blog with a little more detail if you want to find out more and keep checking back because I'm going to be adding more posts about Portugal I'm also working on a Portugal pocket guide that will be full of all my tips and research and recommendations so I'll announce that here on my channel when it's ready I hope you found this video helpful let me know in the comments when your upcoming trip to Portugal is thank you so much for watching and I'll see you in the next one [Music]

videos.transcript: Porto is a joy to visit! Even solo. See what I covered in 3 days, with day trips, on my first visit to Porto, a MUST visit UNESCO World Heritage city on the Douro River. If you’re planning your first visit to Porto, take advantage of all of Porto resources I have for you, including upcoming Porto videos, especially the 'Know Before You Go' travel guide, my Porto trip links below for your easy booking, or just save a ton of time and download one of my ready-made itineraries with travel guides and links, ideal in getting the “best of Porto” in a short time. Subscribe to CORR Travel and follow along for more fantastic itineraries, guides, and tips to inspire your travel. Now, take a spin with me for a few days in and around Porto! [Music] After spending a few days in Madrid, I flew into Porto in mid-October for 4 nights and 3 days to mark the start of my Portugal solo trip. From the airport I quickly caught the metro to my Baixa district accommodations in time to do some quick exploration with a sunset over the Douro River, and grab a light meal on the Casa Guides rooftop before turning in early and save my energy for Porto’s hills the next day. [Music] My visit to Portugal was a mix of vacation and getting ideas where to live in Portugal, so I got a late morning start. Luckily, many of Porto's historic landmarks and sites were close by so I could still see a lot on my first full day in Porto. I had no real agenda. Thankfully, the weather was incredible so I could take advantage of being outside to view the city center, which is on a steep incline. Prepared with good walking shoes, I started at Baixa’s higher elevation and made my way downhill throughout the day to just explore the picturesque neighborhoods, lively dining and shopping streets, and the well-preserved, scenic Ribeira district and promenade, part of the UNESCO designation, where you can dine, or catch a Douro River cruise if you have the time. One particular item I did want to do was sample port in Vila Nova de Gaia, the port production area of Porto. After crossing the Luis I Bridge, also part of the UNESCO designation, I meandered the Avenida de Ramos Pinto enjoying the classic views of Porto, vendor shopping, and noting the multiple Gaia port houses that line the Avenida, which you can stop into or visit as part of a port tasting tour. I landed at Porto Cruz, which was recommended to me, sitting outside for a late afternoon 5-port tasting with nibbles, while people watching, as the sun started to set. The views of Porto cannot be beat at this time of day from Gaia. Forgetting the time, I failed to catch the Teleférico de Gaia that stops at 6, so I walked across river to the top of the bridge, and headed back to Baixa to find a light dinner and another early night in to rest for a long excursion tomorrow. [Music] Day two started earlier with an 8am pick up to begin a small group tour of another UNESCO World Heritage site: the Alto Douro Wine Region, and THE highlight of my Porto stay. Trust me, this is a MUST DO from Porto, which I recommend booking in advance. In just over an hour, the four of us, including Pedro, our tour guide, were stopping to see sweeping views of the terraced valley and birthplace of port wine and learning about Porto’s wine making history. We then drove into Pinhão for a short coffee break before departing on our 1-hour boat ride on the Douro River. We are in the heart of port wine making country, and it is absolutely georgous. It's October. It's a little bit chilly. I'm sure it's going to warm up. It's still in the morning, so... This is grand. We're getting ready to get on the boat and take a cruise down the river. [Music] Another day of awesome weather allowed for clear visibility of the hillside, vineyard-set quintas that offer wine tastings and accommodations. After the river cruise, was an authentic Portuguese lunch with wine at a small family restaurant, learning more about the local cuisine and port from Pedro, and viewing some of the oldest tiles in Portugal depicting the region’s history at the Pinhão train station. Then, it was off to two wine houses for tours, tastings, with locally made food pairings, while overlooking the river. This Douro Valley day trip was unforgettable, and it still left some time to see more of Porto upon return. [Music] I opted to forgo the whole day in Porto on my last day because I really wanted to see more of Portugal beyond Porto. Thankfully, Porto is ideally situated for day trips, from historical and UNESCO sites, to beach towns, and more wine tasting. Another epic weather day, I, instead, walked more around Porto. [Music] Then I was off to the São Bento station, taking in the intricate artwork of the iconic blue tiled walls before buying an inexpensive round trip ticket for my self-guided day trip to: Braga! In an hour, I was arriving in Braga and within minutes passing through the Porta Nova Arch to explore this quaint Portuguese town and gateway to the Peneda-Gerês National Park and Vinho Verde wine region, one of my favorite wines. I had read about Casa de Pasto Carvalheiras on a Portugal website, so this is where I had a late lunch of petiscos, local Vinho Verde, and desert on the patio. It was the perfect respite. Lovely staff and incredible food, I definitely recommend stopping here for lunch! Then off to walk more of the city center... [Music] and then time for a little more crispy Vinho Verde with a traditional egg yolk pudding at Dona Petista. Why not? Before riding the train back to Porto in time for my last entry ticket to the 18th century baroque Clérigos Church and climb the bell tower’s 225 steps before it closed for 360 city views, and one last sunset over Porto, the perfect way to end a 3-day stay. [Music] I may not have spent every day in Porto, but I still had a wonderful time. My centrally located hostel was ideal for my budget stay; easily getting from the airport by metro; seeing everything I wanted to in Porto on foot; being situated for my tour pickup and close to the São Bento station for my Braga day trip - both of which nicely complimented my time in Porto; and, catching a ride out of Porto to my next Portugal destination: Coimbra! [Music]

videos.description:
Planning your first trip to Porto? After spending a week exploring the city, I’ve pulled together a practical 3-day itinerary with all the best things to do, food suggestions, and the spots that IMO are worth your time, without trying to cram too much in. 
You’ll wander through Ribeira, cross over to Vila Nova de Gaia for port tasting, cruise along the river, and even head out to the Douro Valley for a day trip. I’ve also included plenty of useful tips along the way, so hopefully it helps you plan your trip.

✍️📍 Free Map + Written Itinerary: https://ourtravelplace.co.uk/how-to-spend-3-days-in-porto/

✍️ Read more things to do in Porto: https://ourtravelplace.co.uk/things-to-do-in-porto-portugal/

📽️ Watch more things to do in Porto: https://youtu.be/DBEO5BVsrg4

*This video description contains affiliate links (marked with an asterisk) that help support this channel at no extra cost to you.

✅ PORTO ACTIVITIES:
🔗 Porto Walkers Free Walking Tour: https://www.portowalkers.pt/
🔗 Palácio da Bolsa: https://palaciodabolsa.com/
🔗 *Six Bridges River Cruise: https://getyourguide.tpx.gr/7uU0Ftvn
🔗 *Taylor’s Port Lodge Tour & Tasting + WoW Combination Ticket: https://getyourguide.tpx.gr/Z83CAZR2

videos.transcript: Porto is an incredible city with loads to see and do. So, if you're spending 3 days here, it helps to have a bit of a plan. After recently spending a very enjoyable week in Porto, this is how we'd spend 3 days if it was our first visit. Hopefully, by the end of this video, [music] you'll have a few great ideas for your own trip. >> [music] >> Start your day with coffee and a pastry or two. I've added some options for places to go to a map and written guide. The one I'm showing you now is Confeitaria do Bolhão, which is the oldest pastry shop in the city and has a ton of delicious sweet and savory options. This used to be the place where rich people from Porto would have breakfast before going to Bolhão Market, which we'll be visiting a bit later. Get your first feel for Porto on a free walking tour led by a local. They'll guide you through the historic streets, show you some of the sights, >> [music] >> share folk stories, and give tips on where to eat and drink. The tour can last up to 3 hours and covers the eastern part of the city center, finishing at the river fronts. I've pulled everything in this video into a map and written itinerary in case that's useful. You can grab it using the QR code on screen or the link in the video description. You might not want to leave the beautiful Ribeira waterfront just yet, but you'll be back later. For lunch, head to Mercado do Bolhão, Porto's oldest and most famous markets. Wander the bustling stalls and grab small bites of food from different vendors. Pair it with a fresh juice or a glass of green wine, a delicious Portuguese wine that's light, fruity, and just a little bit fizzy. Just a 3-minute walk from Bolhão Market is the 18th century Chapel of Souls, which is completely covered [music] in blue and white tiles. Hopefully, you can tell from these clips that it's well [music] worth a short detour. Spend the rest of your afternoon exploring the colorful Ribeira neighborhood. You'll have had a taste of it during the walking tour, but make time to wander at your own pace and see what you find. The area is full of character with its narrow streets packed with brightly colored buildings. Make sure you stop by Praça da Ribeira, >> [music] >> one of the oldest parts of the city and historically the heart of Porto's river trade. By now, you might be flagging a bit and the Six Bridges River Cruise is the perfect excuse to have a sit down. You'll cruise along the Douro, enjoy the views, listen to the audio guide, and probably wave manically at every boat that passes you, which I'm pretty sure is tourist boat's law. It's usually a couple of euros cheaper to take from the Gaia side rather than the Ribeira side of the river, so it's up to you whether that saving is worth crossing the bridge for. That evening, go to a traditional fado show. Fado is the style of Portuguese music that's all about emotion and storytelling. It's a big part of Portugal's national identity, so in Porto, you'll find loads of places offering performances from classic fado houses to dinner shows. >> [singing] [music] >> If you don't go to a dinner show and you're looking for somewhere to eat, >> [music] >> a good tip is to check TheFork. You can often find really big discounts, sometimes up to 50%. One night, we had bread with dips, clams in white wine, a seafood risotto, and cheesecake for dessert, all for under 60 euros for two people. >> [music] >> For breakfast, grab yourself a pastel de nata or two. These little Portuguese custard tarts are dangerously good. They started in Lisbon, but you'll find them in bakeries all over Porto. If you went on the walking tour yesterday, you probably saw the fortress-like cathedral from the outside, but I would recommend returning this morning to see it from the inside. The highlights for us was the Gothic cloister covered in blue and white Portuguese tiles depicting religious scenes. You can also get some great views over Porto from the tower. Take a 30-minute tour of Palácio da Bolsa, originally built in the 1800s as Porto's stock exchange. You'll be shown some amazing rooms with the [music] Arab Room being particularly impressive. Even if you book online, you don't get a time slot. You still need to go to the ticket office to find out [music] the next available tour. We didn't book ahead, went within an hour of opening, >> [music] >> and didn't have to wait long for the next English tour. If you do have a bit of time to wait like we did, you could pass some time by heading around the corner to watch the old [music] trams and view a couple of nearby churches. Before this afternoon's activities, I'd recommend a good filling lunch. And filling is definitely one way to describe the Francesinha, one of Porto's most famous dishes. This gloriously over-the-top beast of a sandwich is layered with meat, melted cheese, and a rich beer-based sauce. It usually also comes with chips and sometimes even an egg on top. Don't worry, vegetarians, some places do do vegetarian versions as well. If that sounds a bit much, then fair enough. You could try a Cachorrinho instead. This is Porto's take on a hot dog, small, crispy, cheesy, and finished with a spicy sauce. Still far from healthy, but another Porto favorite that isn't as intense as the Francesinha. For one of the best views in Porto, walk across the top of Dom Luís I Bridge. Just watch out for the metro. The views over the Douro River and the colorful Ribeira district are fantastic and it's a great spot for photos. This double-decker bridge was designed by a pupil of Gustave Eiffel. Once you've crossed the bridge, you've actually left Porto and entered Vila Nova de Gaia, the area directly opposite the city and home to the famous port wine cellars. Spend some time wandering around Gaia, especially along the waterfront. It's a lovely area with a relaxed [music] feel, street musicians, market stalls, and the cable car gliding overhead. Keep an eye out for the large recycled materials. If you've seen Amadora videos, this was made by the same artist who created the seal mural and the fish sculpture. Gaia is also home to the World of Wine complex, which has several museums dedicated to wine, chocolates, cork, and regional history. We chose a ticket that included Taylor's Port Lodge and tasting plus entry to one museum of our choice. The World of Wine staff we spoke to recommended the Wine Experience, so that's the one we went for. It's pretty interactive, walks you through how wine is made, and there's even a short tasting demo at the end. I would recommend it. If you do book the World of Wine combo tickets, follow your museum visit with a tour and tasting at Taylor's. It's self-guided, so you can explore at your own pace before sampling a white, tawny, and a vintage in the beautiful garden. There are plenty of other lodges you could visit around Gaia, so I've added a few other options to the map and written itinerary. End your day at Jardim do Morro. It's my favorite viewpoint in Porto with a fantastic view across the Douro towards Ribeira and the colorful buildings climbing up the hillside. In the evening, the atmosphere here is usually really lively. You'll often find live music, groups of friends and family gathering on the grass, and people settling in to watch the sunsets. There's often vendors selling drinks and snacks, too, although you could also just bring your own. Even if you don't want to stay until sunset, it's still a really nice place to spend a bit of time at any point in the day. For your third day, I'd really recommend out to the Douro Valley because it was one of the highlights of our trip. Of course, you could easily spend another day exploring Porto itself, so I'll link a video and written guide in the description with more things to do in the city if you'd prefer to stay local. The Douro Valley is Portugal's most famous wine region. We chose to visit as part of a guided [music] tour that included transport, a boat trip, visits to family-run wineries, a tasty lunch, and, of course, wine tasting. [music] After we were picked up and driven out to the Douro Valley, our day began with a 1-hour private river cruise on a small boat, followed [music] by visits to two wineries. We were taken behind the scenes, strolled through the vineyards, and later relaxed by the pool [music] with incredible views across the valley. There were at least 11 different wines to try throughout the day, from the boat trip through to the final winery, and you could sample as much or as little as you like, including refills. While the trip was informative, for us it struck a really nice balance between learning and simply enjoying the moments. It felt just as much about soaking up the scenery as it did about tasting the wine. >> [music] >> An easy way to get from Porto Airport to the city center is by metro. Line E runs directly to Trindade, which connects you to the rest of the network. Most places in the center are walkable, but anything further out you can use the metro or buses. You'll need an Andante card, which is a rechargeable travel card you can buy from the vending machines in any metro station, including the airport. Just remember to tap it at the yellow machines before each journey. You'll need one per person, and don't throw them away because you can top them back up. If you got this far, then thank you for watching. I really do hope this video was helpful.
